section.title: The Appointed Feasts
section.sectionRationale: This chapter gathers Israel's entire liturgical calendar, the weekly Sabbath and the seven annual feasts, into a single sweep, each rehearsing God's past redemption and, as the New Testament will show, anticipating stages of Christ's completed work.
section.thesis: God appoints a calendar of sacred times, weekly Sabbath, Passover, Unleavened Bread, Firstfruits, Weeks, Trumpets, the Day of Atonement, and Tabernacles, structuring Israel's whole year around rehearsed remembrance of His redemption and anticipation of its completion.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the weekly Sabbath; Passover and Unleavened Bread; the sheaf of Firstfruits and the counting to the Feast of Weeks; the Feast of Trumpets; the Day of Atonement; and the Feast of Tabernacles, closing the religious year.

units.label: The appointed feasts, and the weekly Sabbath

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and say to them, ‘These are My {{moed|appointed feasts}}, the feasts of the LORD that you are to proclaim as sacred assemblies.
For six days work may be done, but the seventh day is a Sabbath of complete rest, a day of sacred assembly. You must not do any work; wherever you live, it is a Sabbath to the LORD.
These are the LORD’s appointed feasts, the sacred assemblies you are to proclaim at their appointed times.

units.label: Passover and Unleavened Bread
units.note: the year begins with redemption remembered

verses.text: The Passover to the LORD begins at twilight on the fourteenth day of the first month.
On the fifteenth day of the same month begins the Feast of Unleavened Bread to the LORD. For seven days you must eat unleavened bread.
On the first day you are to hold a sacred assembly; you are not to do any regular work.
For seven days you are to present an offering made by fire to the LORD. On the seventh day there shall be a sacred assembly; you must not do any regular work.’”

units.label: Firstfruits, and the counting to the Feast of Weeks
units.note: the sheaf waved, fifty days counted

verses.text: And the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and say, ‘When you enter the land that I am giving you and you reap its harvest, you are to bring to the priest a sheaf of the {{bikkurim|firstfruits}} of your harvest.
And he shall wave the sheaf before the LORD so that it may be accepted on your behalf; the priest is to wave it on the day after the Sabbath.
On the day you wave the sheaf, you shall offer a year-old lamb without blemish as a burnt offering to the LORD,
along with its grain offering of two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil, an offering made by fire to the LORD, a pleasing aroma, and its drink offering of a quarter hin of wine.
You must not eat any bread or roasted or new grain until the very day you have brought this offering to your God. This is to be a permanent statute for the generations to come, wherever you live.
From the day after the Sabbath, the day you brought the sheaf of the wave offering, you are to count off seven full weeks.
You shall count off fifty days until the day after the seventh Sabbath, and then present an offering of new grain to the LORD.
Bring two loaves of bread from your dwellings as a wave offering, each made from two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour, baked with leaven, as the firstfruits to the LORD.

Along with the bread you are to present seven unblemished male lambs a year old, one young bull, and two rams. They will be a burnt offering to the LORD, together with their grain offerings and drink offerings, an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
You shall also prepare one male goat as a sin offering and two male lambs a year old as a peace offering.
The priest is to wave the lambs as a wave offering before the LORD, together with the bread of the firstfruits. The bread and the two lambs shall be holy to the LORD for the priest.
On that same day you are to proclaim a sacred assembly, and you must not do any regular work. This is to be a permanent statute wherever you live for the generations to come.

When you reap the harvest of your land, do not reap all the way to the edges of your field or gather the gleanings of your harvest. Leave them for the poor and the foreign resident. I am the LORD your God.’”
The LORD also said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and say, ‘On the first day of the seventh month you are to have a day of rest, a sacred assembly announced by trumpet blasts.
You must not do any regular work, but you are to present an offering made by fire to the LORD.’”
Again the LORD said to Moses,
“The tenth day of this seventh month is the Day of Atonement. You shall hold a sacred assembly and humble yourselves, and present an offering made by fire to the LORD.
On this day you are not to do any work, for it is the Day of Atonement, when atonement is made for you before the LORD your God.
If anyone does not humble himself on this day, he must be cut off from his people.
I will destroy from among his people anyone who does any work on this day.
You are not to do any work at all. This is a permanent statute for the generations to come, wherever you live.
It will be a {{shabbaton|Sabbath of complete rest}} for you, and you shall humble yourselves. From the evening of the ninth day of the month until the following evening you are to keep your Sabbath.”

units.label: The Feast of Tabernacles
units.note: dwelling in booths, rejoicing before the LORD

media.alt: Temporary shelters of branches under an open sky
media.caption: Israel dwells in booths for seven days, remembering the wilderness and rejoicing before the LORD.

verses.text: And the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and say, ‘On the fifteenth day of the seventh month the Feast of {{sukkot|Tabernacles}} to the LORD begins, and it continues for seven days.
On the first day there shall be a sacred assembly. You must not do any regular work.

For seven days you are to present an offering made by fire to the LORD. On the eighth day you are to hold a sacred assembly and present an offering made by fire to the LORD. It is a solemn assembly; you must not do any regular work.

These are the LORD’s appointed feasts, which you are to proclaim as sacred assemblies for presenting offerings by fire to the LORD, burnt offerings and grain offerings, sacrifices and drink offerings, each on its designated day.
These offerings are in addition to the offerings for the LORD’s Sabbaths, and in addition to your gifts, to all your vow offerings, and to all the freewill offerings you give to the LORD.

On the fifteenth day of the seventh month, after you have gathered the produce of the land, you are to celebrate a feast to the LORD for seven days. There shall be complete rest on the first day and also on the eighth day.

On the first day you are to gather the fruit of majestic trees, the branches of palm trees, and the boughs of leafy trees and of willows of the brook. And you are to rejoice before the LORD your God for seven days.
You are to celebrate this as a feast to the LORD for seven days each year. This is a permanent statute for the generations to come; you are to celebrate it in the seventh month.
You are to dwell in booths for seven days. All the native-born of Israel must dwell in booths,
so that your descendants may know that I made the Israelites dwell in booths when I brought them out of the land of Egypt. I am the LORD your God.’”
So Moses announced to the Israelites the appointed feasts of the LORD.

moed.gloss: Appointed time or appointed feast, from a root meaning to fix or designate. These are not spontaneous holidays but fixed dates God Himself sets on Israel's calendar, structuring their whole year around remembering His redemption.

bikkurim.gloss: Firstfruits, the earliest and best portion of the harvest, offered before the rest could be used, acknowledging that the whole harvest, and the land itself, belongs to God. Paul later applies this exact term to Christ's resurrection, 1 Corinthians 15:20.

shabbaton.gloss: A Sabbath of complete rest, an intensified form of the ordinary word for Sabbath. Applied here to the Day of Atonement, it marks that day as carrying the same total cessation of work as the weekly Sabbath itself.

sukkot.gloss: Booths or temporary shelters, giving the Feast of Tabernacles its name. Dwelling in these fragile structures for seven days rehearsed Israel's wilderness dependence on God and anticipated, in John's Gospel, the Word becoming flesh and tabernacling among us.

basic.context: This chapter lists Israel's entire yearly calendar of sacred times: the weekly Sabbath, Passover, Unleavened Bread, the Firstfruits sheaf, the Feast of Weeks fifty days later, the Feast of Trumpets, the Day of Atonement, and the Feast of Tabernacles.
basic.hermeneutics: This is Israel's specific old covenant festival calendar. Colossians 2:16-17 explicitly calls these feasts a shadow of what was to come, whose substance belongs to Christ; Christians are not obligated to observe them, though the pattern of structured, communal worship remembering God's redemption continues and finds its center in Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: Each mo'ed, appointed time, is fixed by God, not chosen by Israel. The bikkurim, firstfruits, wave offering anticipates the full harvest to come, and Paul uses this exact term of Christ's resurrection. The sukkot, booths, of the final feast recall the wilderness and anticipate God's dwelling with His people.
basic.typology: Passover, Unleavened Bread, Firstfruits, and Weeks are explicitly interpreted by the New Testament as fulfilled in Christ's death, sinless purity, resurrection, and the Spirit's outpouring; Tabernacles anticipates both the incarnation and the final ingathering when God's dwelling will be with man forever.
basic.christ: Paul writes plainly, Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed, 1 Corinthians 5:7, and calls Him the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep, 1 Corinthians 15:20. Acts 2 records the Spirit poured out at the very feast of Weeks, fifty days after the resurrection, gathering the first great harvest of souls into the church.
basic.god: God is a God of appointed times, not disorder; He structures His people's whole year around remembering His redemption from Egypt and anticipating its final completion, showing that worship is meant to shape all of time, not merely isolated moments.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 5:7-8 calls Christ our Passover. 1 Corinthians 15:20-23 names Him the firstfruits of the resurrection. Acts 2:1-41 records Pentecost's fulfillment. John 7:37-39 shows Jesus offering living water at the Feast of Tabernacles. Revelation 21:3 promises God's dwelling will finally be with man.
basic.oneStory: Israel's whole year was structured to rehearse God's redemption and lean forward to its completion. Every feast finds its true content in Christ: our Passover, our unleavened purity, our firstfruits from the dead, our Pentecost outpouring, and the coming day when God's dwelling with His people, pictured in booths, becomes permanent and complete.

context.literary: This chapter gathers into one sweep the calendar implied by scattered earlier references, moving from the weekly Sabbath through the spring feasts of Passover season to the harvest feasts of the seventh month, framing Israel's whole religious year.
context.historical: As an agricultural and pastoral people entering Canaan, Israel's feasts were tied to the actual harvest calendar even as they commemorated historical events, uniting the rhythms of the land with the story of redemption from Egypt.
context.canonical: This calendar becomes the framework the Gospels and Acts repeatedly use to date and interpret the events of Christ's death, resurrection, and the giving of the Spirit, since each occurred at, or in explicit relation to, one of these appointed feasts.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish a fixed liturgical calendar giving concrete shape to Israel's ongoing memory of redemption from Egypt and its regular worship of God at the tabernacle.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter prescribes Israel's specific old covenant festival calendar. Colossians 2:16-17 explicitly teaches that these feasts were a shadow of what was to come, the substance belonging to Christ; Christians are not bound to observe them, though the underlying pattern, structured worship remembering God's saving work, continues in the church's own rhythms, now centered on Christ.

debates.question: How exact and detailed should Christians be in mapping each feast to a New Testament fulfillment?

views.label: Confident, textually anchored typology for feasts the New Testament explicitly interprets
views.case: Passover, Unleavened Bread, Firstfruits, and Weeks are directly and explicitly connected to Christ's death, sinless life, resurrection, and the Spirit's outpouring by the New Testament authors themselves, so these connections rest on apostolic authority, not speculation.

views.label: More caution for feasts without explicit New Testament interpretation
views.case: Trumpets and Tabernacles are not assigned an explicit New Testament fulfillment event the way the spring feasts are; while broad themes, the trumpet call at Christ's return, God's dwelling with man, fit naturally, interpreters should hold detailed timelines built on the fall feasts more loosely, since Scripture itself does not make these connections explicit.

debates.bearing: Confidence should track the New Testament's own explicit interpretive statements; where Scripture directly identifies a fulfillment, that reading carries real authority, and where it does not, believers should hold suggested connections as reasonable but not certain.

study.originalLanguages: Each mo'ed, appointed time, is fixed by God's own designation, not Israel's convenience, giving the whole chapter its name, the appointed feasts of the LORD. Bikkurim, firstfruits, names both the literal sheaf waved at harvest and, in Paul's hands, Christ's own resurrection. Sukkot, booths, gives the final feast its name and its meaning: dwelling exposed and dependent, as Israel once did in the wilderness.

typology.type: Passover, the fourteenth day of the first month
typology.fulfillment: Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed, 1 Corinthians 5:7, fulfilled at the very Passover season in Jerusalem.

typology.type: The Feast of Unleavened Bread, seven days without leaven
typology.fulfillment: Pictures a life cleared of the leaven of malice and sin, lived out with sincerity and truth because Christ, our Passover, is sacrificed, 1 Corinthians 5:7-8.

typology.type: The sheaf of firstfruits, waved the day after the Sabbath
typology.fulfillment: Christ, raised on the first day of the week, is Himself the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep, guaranteeing the resurrection harvest to follow, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23.

typology.type: The Feast of Weeks, fifty days later, with new grain and leavened loaves
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled when the Spirit is poured out at Pentecost, exactly fifty days after the resurrection, gathering the first great harvest of souls into the church, Acts 2:1-41.

typology.type: The Feast of Tabernacles, dwelling in temporary shelters
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates both the incarnation, when the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, John 1:14, and the final ingathering when God's dwelling will be with man forever, Revelation 21:3.

study.christ: The New Testament reads Israel's spring feasts as directly fulfilled in Christ: He is our Passover lamb sacrificed, 1 Corinthians 5:7, the unleavened purity His people are called to live out, 1 Corinthians 5:8, and the firstfruits of the resurrection harvest, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23. Fifty days after His resurrection, at the very feast of Weeks, the Spirit is poured out in Acts 2, gathering the first great harvest of souls exactly when Israel's calendar pointed forward to a harvest offering.
study.god: God is a God of order and appointed times, not disorder; He fixes Israel's whole calendar around remembering His redemption from Egypt and anticipating its completion, showing that true worship is meant to shape all of time, not merely isolated religious moments. His faithfulness to keep every appointed feast's typological promise, in Christ's death, resurrection, and the Spirit's outpouring, displays His faithfulness to keep every promise He makes.

crossReferences.note: Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed; believers are called to keep the feast with sincerity and truth, unleavened by malice.
Christ is the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep, guaranteeing the resurrection of all who belong to Him.
The Spirit is poured out at Pentecost, the Feast of Weeks, fifty days after the resurrection, birthing the church.
These feasts are called a shadow of what was to come; the substance belongs to Christ.

study.oneStory: Israel's whole year was structured to rehearse God's past redemption and lean forward toward its completion. Every feast finds its true content in Christ: our Passover sacrificed, our unleavened purity secured, our firstfruits raised from the dead, our Pentecost harvest of souls gathered by the Spirit, and, still ahead, the day when God's dwelling with His people, first pictured in fragile booths, becomes permanent and complete.

quiz.q: How many days did the weekly cycle include before the Sabbath of complete rest, according to verse 3?

options.text: Six days of work, followed by a seventh day Sabbath of complete rest.
options.note: Verse 3 states this pattern explicitly.

options.text: Ten days of work, followed by one day of rest.
options.note: The text specifies a six day and seventh day pattern, not a ten day cycle.

options.text: No regular weekly pattern is described.
options.note: Verse 3 explicitly describes the six and seven day pattern.

options.text: Three days of work, followed by four days of rest.
options.note: Verse 3 specifies six days of work and one day of rest.

quiz.q: When was the sheaf of firstfruits waved before the LORD, according to verse 11?

options.text: On the day after the Sabbath, during the Feast of Unleavened Bread.
options.note: Verse 11 specifies this timing directly.

options.text: Exactly fifty days after Passover.
options.note: Fifty days describes the timing of the Feast of Weeks, not the firstfruits sheaf, which came earlier.

options.text: At the very end of the agricultural year.
options.note: The firstfruits offering came near the start of harvest, not its end.

options.text: No specific timing is given.
options.note: Verse 11 gives a specific timing for this offering.

quiz.q: How many days after the firstfruits sheaf was the Feast of Weeks to be celebrated?

options.text: Fifty days.
options.note: Verse 16 specifies counting fifty days until this feast.

options.text: Seven days.
options.note: Seven days describes the length of Unleavened Bread and Tabernacles, not the interval to the Feast of Weeks.

options.text: One hundred days.
options.note: Verse 16 specifies fifty days, not one hundred.

options.text: No interval is specified.
options.note: Verses 15-16 specify this fifty day count explicitly.

quiz.q: What were the Israelites commanded to do for seven days during the Feast of Tabernacles?

options.text: Dwell in booths and rejoice before the LORD, remembering their time in the wilderness.
options.note: Verses 40-43 specify this practice and its stated purpose.

options.text: Fast completely for the entire seven days.
options.note: Fasting or humbling is specifically associated with the Day of Atonement, not Tabernacles, which involves rejoicing.

options.text: Remain silent and avoid all public gathering.
options.note: The text specifically calls for sacred assemblies and rejoicing, not silence or isolation.

options.text: No specific practice is commanded for this feast.
options.note: Verses 40-43 give specific and detailed instructions.

quiz.q: How does 1 Corinthians 15:20-23 connect Christ to the Feast of Firstfruits?

options.text: It calls Christ the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep, guaranteeing the resurrection of all who belong to Him.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 15:20-23 makes this connection directly.

options.text: It denies any connection between Christ and the Old Testament feasts.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 15:20-23 draws this connection explicitly rather than denying it.

options.text: It applies the firstfruits language only to Old Testament harvests, never to Christ.
options.note: Paul explicitly applies this very term to Christ's own resurrection.

options.text: There is no New Testament reference to firstfruits at all.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 15:20 uses this exact term of Christ.

questions: God structured Israel's entire year around remembering His redemption. What regular rhythms, daily, weekly, yearly, shape your own remembrance of what God has done for you in Christ?

Passover looked back to a specific historical rescue; the church now looks back to a specific historical resurrection. How present and vivid is Easter, not just as a date but as a lived reality, in your ordinary week to week faith?

The Feast of Weeks required Israel to wait, count, and anticipate before the harvest offering came. Where in your life is God asking you to wait faithfully for something you cannot yet see or receive?

Tabernacles called Israel to dwell in fragile, temporary shelters, remembering their dependence on God in the wilderness. What comforts or securities in your life would be hardest to live without for even a week?

The whole festal calendar leans forward to a future, final ingathering when God's dwelling will be permanently with His people. How does that future hope shape the way you face the hardships of today?

translationNotes.note: The day after the Sabbath became a point of significant later debate among Jewish interpreters, some understanding it as the day after the weekly Sabbath during Unleavened Bread week, others as the day after the first day of the feast itself, which affected the calculated date of the Feast of Weeks.

Announced by trumpet blasts translates a phrase, zikron teruah, that can also be rendered a memorial of trumpet blasts or a shout, giving this feast its traditional name, though the text does not explain what the trumpets specifically commemorated.

The fruit of majestic trees and branches gathered on the first day of Tabernacles later developed, in Jewish tradition, into the specific set of four species carried in procession, though the text itself leaves the exact identification of some plants open.
